findings.description: Connection from 10.128.1.13 to 192.168.220.132 on port 3389 (RDP). This port is commonly associated with attacks or services that should be monitored.

metadata.technical_summary: A connection from 10.128.1.13 to 192.168.220.132 on port 3389 (RDP) indicates suspicious network activity that may be associated with attacks or unauthorized access.

best_control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Disabled: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users

control.control_id: 2.3.10.4 (L1) Ensure 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is set to 'Disabled' (Automated)
control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Disabled: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users

control.control_id: 2.3.10.3 (L1) Ensure 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' is set to 'Enabled' (MS only) (Automated)
control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Enabled: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares
Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.

control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\WindowsFirewall\DomainProfile:DefaultInboundAction
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Block (default): Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall with Advanced Security\Windows Firewall Properties\Domain Profile\Inbound connections
Ensure 'Windows Firewall: Public: Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'

remediation.executive_summary: Suspicious RDP activity from an unknown IP address (10.128.1.13) to an internal server (192.168.220.132) on port 3389 suggests potential unauthorized access or reconnaissance. This remediation plan focuses on hardening Windows Server network access and firewall configurations to prevent anonymous access and restrict inbound connections, thereby reducing the attack surface for RDP-related attacks. Implementing these steps will help mitigate brute-force attempts and unauthorized lateral movement.

steps.title: Harden Network Access: Disable 'Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: This setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group, which could grant them unintended permissions and aid in reconnaissance or privilege escalation. Disabling this reduces the attack surface for anonymous access, making it harder for attackers to gain initial footholds or gather information.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open the Group Policy Management Editor (or Local Security Policy Editor if applying locally).
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options'.
Locate the policy 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'.
Double-click the policy, select the 'Disabled' radio button, and click 'OK'.
You should see the policy status change to 'Disabled' in the policy list.

steps.title: Harden Network Access: Enable 'Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' to 'Enabled'.
steps.explanation: Enabling this policy prevents anonymous users from enumerating Security Account Manager (SAM) accounts and network shares. This significantly hinders attackers attempting to gather information about user accounts and shared resources, which is often a precursor to brute-force attacks (T1110) or lateral movement.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: In the Group Policy Management Editor (or Local Security Policy Editor), navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options'.
Locate the policy 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'.
Double-click the policy, select the 'Enabled' radio button, and click 'OK'.
You should see the policy status change to 'Enabled' in the policy list.

steps.title: Harden Network Security: Disable 'Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: This setting prevents the LocalSystem account from falling back to a NULL session when attempting to access network resources. Disabling this reduces the risk of information disclosure or unauthorized access via NULL sessions, which can be exploited by attackers to gain information or access.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: In the Group Policy Management Editor (or Local Security Policy Editor), navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options'.
Locate the policy 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'.
Double-click the policy, select the 'Disabled' radio button, and click 'OK'.
You should see the policy status change to 'Disabled' in the policy list.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Domain Profile to Block Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Windows Firewall Properties > Domain Profile > Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Setting the default inbound action for the Domain Profile to 'Block' significantly restricts unsolicited incoming network traffic, including RDP connections, unless explicitly allowed by a specific firewall rule. This is a critical step in preventing unauthorized access and mitigating RDP brute-force attacks (T1110) and lateral movement (T1563.002).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall Properties > Domain Profile
substeps: In the Group Policy Management Editor (or Local Security Policy Editor), navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security'.
Right-click 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' in the left pane and select 'Properties'.
Click on the 'Domain Profile' tab.
Under 'State', locate 'Inbound connections' and select 'Block (default)' from the dropdown menu.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. You should see the 'Inbound connections' setting for the Domain Profile updated to 'Block (default)'.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall Public Profile to Block Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Windows Firewall Properties > Public Profile > Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Similar to the Domain Profile, setting the default inbound action for the Public Profile to 'Block' ensures that when the server is connected to an untrusted or public network, all unsolicited inbound connections, including RDP, are blocked by default. This provides an additional layer of security against external threats and unauthorized RDP access.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall Properties > Public Profile
substeps: In the Group Policy Management Editor (or Local Security Policy Editor), navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security'.
Right-click 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' in the left pane and select 'Properties'.
Click on the 'Public Profile' tab.
Under 'State', locate 'Inbound connections' and select 'Block (default)' from the dropdown menu.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. You should see the 'Inbound connections' setting for the Public Profile updated to 'Block (default)'.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server.
Access to Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) if applying via Group Policy, or Local Security Policy Editor if applying locally.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the Group Policy or local security settings, run 'gpupdate /force' on the target server. Then, open the Local Security Policy Editor (or Group Policy Management Editor) and navigate to the respective UI paths for each policy. Confirm that 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is 'Disabled', 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' is 'Enabled', and 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' is 'Disabled'. For Windows Firewall, navigate to 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' properties and confirm that 'Inbound connections' for both 'Domain Profile' and 'Public Profile' are set to 'Block (default)'.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

remediation.executive_summary: The observed RDP connection indicates potential unauthorized access or suspicious activity, which could lead to credential compromise or lateral movement within the network. This remediation plan focuses on investigating the initial event, hardening Windows Server RDP configurations to prevent similar incidents, and enhancing continuous monitoring to detect future unauthorized remote access attempts. Implementing these steps will reduce the attack surface and improve detection capabilities against remote access threats.

steps.title: Investigate the suspicious RDP connection in Windows Event Logs
steps.command_or_action: Open Event Viewer and review Security logs for RDP-related events.
steps.explanation: This step helps determine the nature and scope of the suspicious RDP connection (T1021.001). By examining login attempts, successful logins, and logoffs, you can identify the user account, source IP, and time of the activity, which is crucial for understanding potential credential access (T1110) or lateral movement (T1563.002, T1563).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security
substeps: Open 'Event Viewer' by searching for it in the Start menu or running 'eventvwr.msc'.
In the left pane, navigate to 'Windows Logs' and then select 'Security'.

Filter the Security log for Event IDs related to RDP logins: 4624 (Successful logon), 4625 (Failed logon), 4634 (Logoff), 4647 (User initiated logoff), and 4776 (Credential Validation). Look for entries around the time of the reported suspicious connection (10.128.1.113 to 192.168.220.241 on port 3389).

Examine the 'Source Network Address' and 'Account Name' fields within these events to identify the origin and user associated with the RDP connection. You should see details confirming or denying the reported connection and associated user activity.

steps.title: Deny RDP logon for Guest accounts via Group Policy
steps.command_or_action: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\User Rights Assignment\Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services
steps.explanation: Restricting RDP access for Guest accounts prevents unauthorized or anonymous users from establishing remote desktop sessions, reducing the attack surface for remote access (T1021.001) and potential credential abuse (T1110).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management' by searching for it in the Start menu or running 'gpmc.msc'.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to your Windows Servers, right-click it, and select 'Edit'.
In the Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'User Rights Assignment'.
Double-click 'Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services'. Check 'Define these policy settings' and ensure 'Guests' is added to the list. Click 'OK'.

Close the Group Policy Management Editor and run 'gpupdate /force' on the target server(s) to apply the policy immediately. You should see a confirmation that the policy update completed successfully.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' via Group Policy
steps.command_or_action: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group, which can limit their ability to enumerate network resources or gain unauthorized access, thereby mitigating potential credential access (T1110) and remote services (T1021.001) attacks.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management' by searching for it in the Start menu or running 'gpmc.msc'.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to your Windows systems, right-click it, and select 'Edit'.
In the Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Security Options'.
Double-click 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'. Select 'Disabled' and click 'OK'.

Close the Group Policy Management Editor and run 'gpupdate /force' on the target system(s) to apply the policy immediately. You should see a confirmation message indicating the policy update was successful.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' via Group Policy
steps.command_or_action: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents services running as LocalSystem from falling back to NULL sessions when authenticating to network resources. This reduces the risk of information disclosure and unauthorized access that could be leveraged for credential access (T1110) or remote services (T1021.001) attacks.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management' by searching for it in the Start menu or running 'gpmc.msc'.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to your Windows Servers, right-click it, and select 'Edit'.
In the Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Security Options'.
Double-click 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'. Select 'Disabled' and click 'OK'.

Close the Group Policy Management Editor and run 'gpupdate /force' on the target server(s) to apply the policy immediately. You should see a message confirming the policy update completed successfully.

steps.title: Implement continuous monitoring for unauthorized RDP connections
steps.command_or_action: Configure Windows Event Log collection and alerting for RDP-related security events.
steps.explanation: Continuous monitoring of RDP activity is critical for detecting and responding to unauthorized remote desktop access (T1021.001), brute force attempts (T1110), or lateral movement (T1563.002, T1563) in real-time. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security > Filter Current Log
substeps: On your Windows Server, open 'Event Viewer' and navigate to 'Windows Logs' > 'Security'.
Right-click 'Security' and select 'Filter Current Log...'. In the 'Event IDs:' field, enter '4624, 4625, 4634, 4776' to focus on RDP login/logoff and credential validation events.

If using a SIEM or centralized logging solution (e.g., Microsoft Sentinel via Azure Monitor Agent), ensure these specific Event IDs from your Windows Servers are being collected and forwarded. Verify that the data connector for Windows Security Events is configured to ingest these logs.

Configure alerts within your SIEM or monitoring system for multiple failed RDP login attempts (Event ID 4625) from a single source IP, or successful RDP logins (Event ID 4624) from unusual source IPs or at unusual times. You should receive a test alert if a simulated suspicious RDP event occurs, confirming the monitoring is active.

prerequisites: Administrative access to Windows Server instances.
Access to Group Policy Management Editor (if managing via GPO).
Access to Event Viewer on target Windows Servers.
Access to your SIEM or centralized logging solution (if applicable).
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the Group Policy settings, verify their application by navigating to the respective UI paths in the Group Policy Management Editor or Local Security Policy on a target server and confirming the settings are 'Disabled' or 'Guests' is added. For monitoring, simulate a failed RDP login and confirm an alert is generated in your monitoring system.

Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. remediation.executive_summary: The observed RDP connection from an unknown source IP (10.128.1.113) to an internal Windows Server (192.168.220.241) on port 3389 indicates potential unauthorized access or brute-force attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001). This remediation plan outlines steps to investigate the suspicious connection, review network logs, and harden Windows Server configurations to prevent anonymous access and information enumeration, thereby reducing the attack surface for such activities.

steps.title: Investigate RDP Connection Logs on Windows Server
steps.command_or_action: Review Windows Security Event Logs for RDP connection attempts.
steps.explanation: This step helps determine if the suspicious RDP connection was successful, which user account was involved, and if there were multiple failed login attempts indicative of brute-force activity (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Start > Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security
substeps: Open "Event Viewer" by searching for it in the Start menu.
In the left pane, navigate to "Windows Logs" and then click on "Security".
Filter the current log for Event IDs "4624" (Successful Logon), "4625" (Failed Logon), and "4648" (A logon was attempted using explicit credentials).
Look for events where the "Source Network Address" matches "10.128.1.113" and the "Logon Type" is "10" (RemoteInteractive - RDP).
Note the "Account Name" for successful logons or "Account For Which Logon Failed" for failed attempts.
You should see detailed event properties showing the source IP, target account, and logon success/failure status.

steps.title: Review Cisco Meraki Firewall Logs for RDP Traffic
steps.command_or_action: Access the Cisco Meraki dashboard to review network traffic logs.
steps.explanation: This step provides network-level visibility into the observed RDP connection, confirming if the traffic traversed the firewall and if any related alerts were generated. This helps in understanding the network path and potential external origin of the brute-force attempt (MITRE ATT&CK T1110).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Meraki dashboard > Network-wide > Monitor > Event log
substeps: Log in to the Cisco Meraki dashboard.
In the left navigation pane, select "Network-wide" then "Monitor", and click on "Event log".
Use the search/filter options to look for traffic from "Source IP: 10.128.1.113" to "Destination IP: 192.168.220.241" on "Destination Port: 3389".
Review the results for any allowed or denied connections matching the criteria, noting timestamps and associated policies.
You should see log entries detailing the RDP connection attempts, including source/destination IPs and ports, and whether the traffic was allowed or blocked by the firewall.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy to disable this setting.
steps.explanation: Disabling this policy prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group, significantly reducing the permissions available to unauthenticated connections. This hardens the system against potential anonymous access exploits that could be used in conjunction with or after initial brute-force attempts (MITRE ATT&CK T1110).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management Console" (GPMC) on a domain controller or a machine with RSAT installed.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to the target Windows Server (192.168.220.241) and "Edit" it.
In the Group Policy Management Editor, expand "Computer Configuration" > "Policies" > "Windows Settings" > "Security Settings" > "Local Policies" > "Security Options".
Locate the policy "Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users".
Double-click the policy, select "Disabled", and then click "Apply" and "OK".
You should see the "Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users" policy now shows "Disabled" in the Group Policy Management Editor.

steps.title: Enable 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares'
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy to enable this setting.
steps.explanation: Enabling this policy prevents anonymous users from enumerating Security Account Manager (SAM) accounts and shared folders. This is a critical step in preventing reconnaissance by attackers, who often enumerate user accounts as a precursor to brute-force or password guessing attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management Console" (GPMC) on a domain controller or a machine with RSAT installed.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to the target Windows Server (192.168.220.241) and "Edit" it.
In the Group Policy Management Editor, expand "Computer Configuration" > "Policies" > "Windows Settings" > "Security Settings" > "Local Policies" > "Security Options".
Locate the policy "Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares".
Double-click the policy, select "Enabled", and then click "Apply" and "OK".
You should see the "Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares" policy now shows "Enabled" in the Group Policy Management Editor.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy to disable this setting.
steps.explanation: Disabling this policy prevents the LocalSystem account from falling back to a NULL session when attempting to access network resources. This reduces the risk of information disclosure or unauthorized access via NULL sessions, which could be leveraged by attackers during reconnaissance or post-exploitation phases, indirectly supporting brute-force efforts (MITRE ATT&CK T1110).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management Console" (GPMC) on a domain controller or a machine with RSAT installed.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to the target Windows Server (192.168.220.241) and "Edit" it.
In the Group Policy Management Editor, expand "Computer Configuration" > "Policies" > "Windows Settings" > "Security Settings" > "Local Policies" > "Security Options".
Locate the policy "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback".
Double-click the policy, select "Disabled", and then click "Apply" and "OK".
You should see the "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback" policy now shows "Disabled" in the Group Policy Management Editor.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server (192.168.220.241).
Access to the Cisco Meraki dashboard.
Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) access if applying changes via GPO.
remediation.verification_procedure: After completing all steps, re-check Windows Event Logs and Meraki logs for any further suspicious RDP activity. Confirm that the specified Group Policy settings are applied and enforced on the target server.

remediation.executive_summary: An observed RDP connection from an unknown source IP (10.128.1.176) to an internal server (192.168.220.132) on port 3389 indicates potential unauthorized access or reconnaissance. This remediation plan outlines steps to investigate the connection, harden Windows Server RDP configurations to reduce the attack surface, and enhance monitoring capabilities to detect and prevent future suspicious RDP activity. Implementing these steps will help mitigate risks associated with brute-force attacks and lateral movement via RDP.

steps.title: Investigate the observed RDP connection on the destination server
steps.command_or_action: Review Windows Event Logs on 192.168.220.132 for RDP connection attempts and authentication events.
steps.explanation: This step is crucial for understanding the nature of the observed RDP connection. By reviewing logs, you can determine if the connection was legitimate, if it was a failed brute-force attempt (T1110), or if it resulted in unauthorized access or lateral movement (T1563.002). This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring and analysis of detected events.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security
substeps: Open 'Event Viewer' on the destination server (192.168.220.132).
Navigate to 'Windows Logs' > 'Security'.
Filter the 'Security' log for Event IDs 4624 (Successful logon) and 4625 (Failed logon) during the time of the observed connection, specifically looking for source IP 10.128.1.176.

Navigate to 'Applications and Services Logs' > 'Microsoft' > 'Windows' > 'TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager' and filter for Event IDs 21 (Remote Desktop Services: Session logon succeeded) and 22 (Remote Desktop Services: Shell start notification) for the relevant timeframe and source IP.

Navigate to 'Applications and Services Logs' > 'Microsoft' > 'Windows' > 'TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager' and filter for Event ID 1149 (Remote Desktop Services: User authentication succeeded) for the relevant timeframe and source IP.
You should see detailed event entries indicating successful or failed RDP login attempts from the source IP, including the user account involved.

steps.title: Ensure Windows Firewall blocks inbound connections on Public profiles
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy to set 'Windows Firewall: Public: Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)'.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections on public network profiles significantly reduces the attack surface, especially for services like RDP. This helps prevent unauthorized remote access attempts (T1563.002) by ensuring that if a server's network profile unexpectedly switches to 'Public', it is not exposed. This directly addresses control 9.3.2.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security - LDAP://...
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management' on a domain controller or the local Group Policy Editor on the server.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' > 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security - LDAP://...'.
In the left pane, click on 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' and then select 'Windows Firewall Properties' in the right-hand 'Actions' pane.
Go to the 'Public Profile' tab and ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'.
Click 'Apply' and 'OK'. You should see the Public Profile's Inbound connections status updated to 'Block (default)'.

steps.title: Disable 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'
steps.command_or_action: Set the Group Policy 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents anonymous users from being treated as members of the 'Everyone' group, which can limit their ability to enumerate system information or access shared resources. This reduces the attack surface for reconnaissance activities that could precede brute-force attacks (T1110) or lateral movement (T1563.002). This directly addresses control 2.3.10.4.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management' on a domain controller or the local Group Policy Editor on the server.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Security Options'.
Locate the policy 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users'.
Double-click the policy, select 'Disabled', and click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
You should see the 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' policy now shows 'Disabled' in the Group Policy Editor.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Set the Group Policy 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents the system from reverting to a NULL session if a LocalSystem account cannot authenticate. This mitigates potential information disclosure vulnerabilities that could be exploited by attackers during reconnaissance phases before attempting brute-force (T1110) or RDP lateral movement (T1563.002). This directly addresses control 2.3.11.2.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management' on a domain controller or the local Group Policy Editor on the server.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Security Options'.
Locate the policy 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'.
Double-click the policy, select 'Disabled', and click 'Apply' and 'OK'.
You should see the 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' policy now shows 'Disabled' in the Group Policy Editor.

steps.title: Configure 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'P-node'
steps.command_or_action: Set the Group Policy 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT NodeType to 'P-node' (Point-to-Point) ensures that NetBIOS name resolution relies primarily on a NetBIOS Name Server (NBNS) like WINS, rather than broadcasting. This reduces network traffic and limits the ability of attackers to perform NetBIOS-based enumeration or spoofing, which could be used in conjunction with brute-force (T1110) or lateral movement (T1563.002) attempts. This addresses control 18.3.4.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management' on a domain controller or the local Group Policy Editor on the server.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Administrative Templates' > 'Network' > 'NetBIOS over TCP/IP'.
Locate the policy 'NetBT NodeType configuration'.
Double-click the policy, select 'Enabled', and then choose 'P-node (recommended)' from the 'Options' dropdown.
Click 'Apply' and 'OK'. You should see the 'NetBT NodeType configuration' policy now shows 'Enabled' with 'P-node (recommended)' in the Group Policy Editor.

steps.title: Review and enhance RDP monitoring and alerting
steps.command_or_action: Ensure RDP login auditing is enabled and consider network-level monitoring on Cisco Meraki MX 250.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring of RDP activity is essential for detecting unauthorized access attempts (T1110) and lateral movement (T1563.002). By ensuring comprehensive logging and setting up alerts, the security team can respond quickly to suspicious RDP connections. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for continuous system monitoring and analysis.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Cisco Meraki dashboard > Network-wide > Event log
substeps: Verify that 'Audit Logon Events' is enabled for both 'Success' and 'Failure' in the Group Policy 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > Audit Policies > Logon/Logoff > Audit Logon'.

On the Cisco Meraki dashboard, navigate to 'Network-wide' > 'Event log' and filter for 'Firewall' or 'Traffic' events related to port 3389 (RDP) to identify any unusual patterns or connections from unexpected sources.

Configure custom alerts in the Cisco Meraki dashboard by navigating to 'Network-wide' > 'Alerts' and creating a new rule to notify administrators of unusual RDP traffic patterns or connections from external IPs to internal RDP ports.
You should see audit policies configured for logon events, and Meraki alerts should be active for RDP traffic anomalies.

prerequisites: Administrative access to affected Windows Servers (192.168.220.132).
Administrative access to a domain controller for Group Policy Management (if applicable).
Administrative access to the Cisco Meraki dashboard.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the remediation steps, monitor Windows Event Logs (Security, TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager, TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager) for any further suspicious RDP activity. Verify that the Group Policy settings are applied by checking the local security policy on the affected server or running 'gpresult /h gpresult.html' and reviewing the report. Confirm that Cisco Meraki alerts are configured and functioning as expected.

findings.title: Connection to Suspicious Port: RDP (3389)
findings.description: Connection from 10.128.1.176 to 10.100.104.132 on port 3389 (RDP). This port is commonly associated with attacks or services that should be monitored.

metadata.technical_summary: A connection from 10.128.1.176 to 10.100.104.132 on port 3389 (RDP) was observed, indicating potential suspicious activity or a service that requires monitoring.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. remediation.executive_summary: An RDP connection from an unknown source (10.128.1.176) to a target server (10.100.104.132) on port 3389 was observed, indicating potential unauthorized access attempts or a misconfigured service. This remediation plan outlines steps to investigate the observed connection, harden the target Windows Server against unauthorized RDP access, and improve overall system monitoring to detect similar activities in the future. Implementing these steps will reduce the attack surface related to RDP and enhance visibility into potential brute-force attacks (T1110, T1110.001).

steps.title: Investigate the Observed RDP Connection on the Target Server
steps.command_or_action: Review Windows Event Logs for RDP connection attempts and successful logins on the target server (10.100.104.132).
steps.explanation: This step helps determine the legitimacy and context of the observed RDP connection. By reviewing security logs, you can identify if the connection was successful, which user account was involved, and if there were any failed login attempts (T1110, T1110.001). This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security
substeps: On the target server (10.100.104.132), open 'Event Viewer' by searching for it in the Start menu.
In the 'Event Viewer' window, navigate to 'Windows Logs' and then select 'Security'.
Filter the logs for Event ID 4624 (Successful Logon) and 4625 (Failed Logon) for the time frame around the observed connection. Look specifically for entries with the source IP address 10.128.1.176.
You should see entries detailing successful or failed RDP login attempts, including the source IP address and the user account involved, or confirmation that no such events occurred.

steps.title: Harden Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: Configure Windows Firewall with Advanced Security via Group Policy to block inbound connections by default for the Domain Profile.
steps.explanation: Implementing this control (9.1.2) ensures that all inbound connections are blocked by default, significantly reducing the attack surface, including for RDP (port 3389). This forces explicit rules for legitimate services, making it harder for attackers to establish connections and mitigating brute-force attempts (T1110, T1110.001).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall Properties > Domain Profile
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management Editor' on a domain controller or a workstation with RSAT installed.

Navigate through the console tree to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' > 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security'.
Right-click on 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' in the left pane and select 'Properties'.
Go to the 'Domain Profile' tab.
Under the 'State' section, ensure that 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. You should see the 'Inbound connections' setting for the Domain Profile now listed as 'Block (default)'.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Configure the 'NetBT NodeType configuration' policy via Group Policy to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'.
steps.explanation: This step implements control 18.3.4 (L1) by configuring NetBIOS over TCP/IP to use P-node. P-node relies on a WINS server for name resolution, reducing broadcast traffic and potentially limiting information disclosure that could be exploited by attackers.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management Editor' on a domain controller or a workstation with RSAT installed.
Navigate through the console tree to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Administrative Templates' > 'Network' > 'NetBIOS over TCP/IP'.
Double-click the policy setting 'NetBT NodeType configuration'.
Select 'Enabled' and then choose 'P-node (recommended)' from the 'Options' dropdown menu.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. You should see the 'State' column for 'NetBT NodeType configuration' now showing 'Enabled'.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: Set the security policy 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled' via Group Policy.
steps.explanation: This step implements control 2.3.11.2 by preventing anonymous access to system resources through NULL sessions. Disabling this fallback reduces the attack surface and prevents potential information disclosure or unauthorized access attempts.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management Editor' on a domain controller or a workstation with RSAT installed.
Navigate through the console tree to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Security Options'.
Locate and double-click the policy setting 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'.
Select 'Disabled'.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. You should see the 'Security Setting' column for 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' now showing 'Disabled'.

steps.title: Review and Restrict RDP Specific Firewall Rules
steps.command_or_action: Review existing RDP inbound rules in Windows Firewall with Advanced Security and restrict allowed source IP addresses to only trusted networks or hosts.
steps.explanation: Following the default block in Step 2, this step ensures that if RDP is required, it is only accessible from explicitly authorized sources. This significantly limits the exposure of RDP to the internet or untrusted internal networks, directly mitigating brute-force attacks (T1110, T1110.001) by reducing the pool of potential attackers.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Inbound Rules
substeps: On the target server (10.100.104.132), open 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' by searching for 'wf.msc' in the Start menu.
In the left pane, select 'Inbound Rules'.
Locate any active 'Remote Desktop (TCP-In)' rules. There might be several, for different profiles or versions.
For each active 'Remote Desktop (TCP-In)' rule, right-click and select 'Properties'. Go to the 'Scope' tab.

Under 'Remote IP address', ensure 'These IP addresses' is selected and add only the specific, authorized source IP addresses or subnets that require RDP access. Remove any 'Any IP address' entries if not explicitly required.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. You should see the RDP inbound rules now explicitly listing allowed remote IP addresses, rather than 'Any'.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server (10.100.104.132).
Administrative access to a Domain Controller or a workstation with Remote Server Administration Tools (RSAT) installed for Group Policy Management.
Understanding of your network's authorized RDP source IP ranges.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the Group Policies, force a Group Policy update on the target server(s) using `gpupdate /force` in an elevated command prompt and then restart the server if necessary. Verify the firewall rules and registry settings are applied as expected. For RDP, attempt to connect from an *unauthorized* IP address; the connection should be blocked. Attempt to connect from an *authorized* IP address; the connection should succeed.

remediation.executive_summary: The finding indicates an RDP connection from an internal IP to another internal IP, which could be suspicious or require better monitoring. This remediation plan focuses on hardening RDP security on Windows Servers by configuring the Windows Firewall to block unsolicited inbound RDP connections, ensuring proper NetBT node type configuration, and disabling NULL session fallback. Additionally, it includes steps to investigate the observed connection and establish ongoing monitoring for RDP activity to detect potential brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001) or unauthorized access.

steps.title: Investigate Observed RDP Connection
steps.command_or_action: Review Windows Event Logs on 10.100.104.132
steps.explanation: This step aims to understand the nature of the RDP connection from 10.128.1.176 to 10.100.104.132. It helps determine if the connection was legitimate, unauthorized, or part of a potential brute-force attempt (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001). This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring and attack detection.
substeps: Log in to the Windows Server at 10.100.104.132 with administrative privileges.
Open "Event Viewer" by searching for it in the Start menu.
Navigate to "Windows Logs" > "Security" and filter for Event ID 4624 (Successful Logon) and 4625 (Failed Logon) for the timeframe around the observed connection. Look for source IP 10.128.1.176.

Additionally, check "Applications and Services Logs" > "Microsoft" > "Windows" > "TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager" > "Operational" for Event ID 21 (Remote Desktop Services: User logoff) and 24 (Remote Desktop Services: User logon succeeded) to confirm RDP session activity.
You should see entries detailing successful or failed RDP login attempts from the source IP, including the user account used.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections by Default
steps.command_or_action: Group Policy Management Editor
steps.explanation: This hardens the Windows Firewall by setting the default inbound action for the Domain Profile to 'Block'. This prevents unsolicited RDP connections and other services from being exposed, reducing the attack surface and mitigating potential brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001). This directly addresses control 9.1.2 (L1) for Windows Firewall.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall Properties > Domain Profile
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management" on a domain controller or a machine with RSAT installed.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to the Organizational Unit (OU) containing the target Windows Servers. Right-click the GPO and select "Edit".

In the Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to "Computer Configuration" > "Policies" > "Windows Settings" > "Security Settings" > "Windows Firewall with Advanced Security" > "Windows Firewall with Advanced Security".
Right-click "Windows Firewall with Advanced Security" in the left pane and select "Properties".
Go to the "Domain Profile" tab. Under "State", locate "Inbound connections" and set it to "Block (default)".
Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "Inbound connections" setting for the Domain Profile now listed as "Block (default)".

steps.title: Set NetBT NodeType Configuration to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Group Policy Management Editor
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT NodeType to P-node (Peer-to-peer) ensures that NetBIOS name resolution primarily relies on a NetBIOS Name Server (NBNS) like WINS, rather than broadcasting. This reduces network traffic and potential information leakage, aligning with control 18.3.4 (L1).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management" on a domain controller or a machine with RSAT installed.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to the Organizational Unit (OU) containing the target Windows Servers. Right-click the GPO and select "Edit".
In the Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to "Computer Configuration" > "Policies" > "Administrative Templates" > "Network" > "NetBIOS over TCP/IP".
Double-click the policy setting "NetBT NodeType configuration".
Select "Enabled" and then choose "P-node (recommended)" from the "Options" dropdown menu.
Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "NetBT NodeType configuration" policy now shows "Enabled" with "P-node (recommended)" in the status column.

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: Group Policy Management Editor
steps.explanation: Disabling 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' prevents services running as LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session when attempting to access network resources. This enhances security by preventing unauthenticated access to system resources, addressing control 2.3.11.2.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management" on a domain controller or a machine with RSAT installed.
Navigate to the appropriate GPO linked to the Organizational Unit (OU) containing the target Windows Servers. Right-click the GPO and select "Edit".
In the Group Policy Management Editor, navigate to "Computer Configuration" > "Policies" > "Windows Settings" > "Security Settings" > "Local Policies" > "Security Options".
Locate and double-click the policy "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback".
Select "Disabled".
Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback" policy now shows "Disabled" in the status column.

steps.title: Implement Ongoing RDP Connection Monitoring and Alerting
steps.command_or_action: Configure Windows Event Forwarding or SIEM integration
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring of RDP connections is crucial for detecting unauthorized access attempts, brute-force attacks (MITRE ATT&CK T1110, T1110.001), and suspicious activity. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 by establishing continuous system monitoring.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Subscriptions (for Event Forwarding) or SIEM Console (e.g., Microsoft Sentinel)
substeps: Configure Event Forwarding (if not using a SIEM): On the target Windows Server (10.100.104.132), open "Event Viewer", navigate to "Subscriptions", and configure a new subscription to forward "Security" logs (Event IDs 4624, 4625) and "TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational" logs (Event IDs 21, 24) to a central log collector.

Configure SIEM Alerts (if applicable): If a Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system is in use (e.g., Microsoft Sentinel), ensure logs from 10.100.104.132 are ingested. Create or verify existing alert rules for: multiple failed RDP login attempts from a single source IP within a short timeframe (indicating brute-force, T1110.001); successful RDP logins from unusual source IPs or at unusual times; and RDP logins using highly privileged accounts.
Test Alerting: Initiate a few failed RDP login attempts from a test machine to 10.100.104.132 and confirm that alerts are triggered in your SIEM or log collection system.
You should receive notifications or see incidents generated in your SIEM/monitoring dashboard for suspicious RDP activity, confirming the monitoring is active.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Windows Server 10.100.104.132.
Administrative access to a Domain Controller or a machine with Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) and Remote Server Administration Tools (RSAT) installed.
Understanding of Group Policy application and inheritance.
Access to your organization's SIEM or log management solution.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the Group Policy settings, force a Group Policy update on the affected servers (`gpupdate /force`) and then verify the settings locally by navigating to the specified UI paths. For monitoring, confirm that alerts are generated in your SIEM or log management system for test cases of suspicious RDP activity.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

remediation.executive_summary: The observed RDP connection from an internal IP to another internal IP on port 3389 indicates potential suspicious activity or a service requiring enhanced monitoring. This remediation plan focuses on investigating the legitimacy of the connection, hardening RDP access controls, and improving system monitoring. These steps will help detect and prevent unauthorized RDP usage, which could be indicative of brute force attacks (T1110) or lateral movement (T1563.002).

steps.title: Investigate RDP Connection Logs
steps.command_or_action: Event Viewer
steps.explanation: Reviewing RDP connection logs helps determine the legitimacy of the observed connection, identify the user account involved, and understand the timing and frequency of access. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 for system monitoring, providing crucial insights into potential unauthorized access attempts (T1110) or lateral movement (T1563.002).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security
substeps: Open "Event Viewer" by typing `eventvwr.msc` in the Run dialog (Win+R) or searching in the Start Menu.
In the left pane, navigate to "Windows Logs" and then select "Security".

Filter the logs for Event ID 4624 (Successful logon) and 4625 (Failed logon) with "Logon Type" 10 (RemoteInteractive) to identify RDP login attempts to the destination IP 192.168.220.132. Specifically, look for the source IP 10.128.1.176.
You should see detailed event properties showing the source IP, destination IP, user account, and logon time for RDP connections, indicating success or failure.

steps.title: Review Existing Windows Firewall Rules for RDP
steps.command_or_action: Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security
steps.explanation: Before making changes, it's crucial to understand the current RDP firewall configuration to ensure that only authorized connections are permitted. Overly permissive rules can expose systems to unauthorized access, which could facilitate brute force attacks (T1110) or lateral movement (T1563.002). This aligns with CIS 9.3.2.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Inbound Rules
substeps: Open "Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security" by searching in the Start Menu or running `wf.msc` from the Run dialog.
In the left pane, click on "Inbound Rules".
Filter or scroll through the rules to find any rules named "Remote Desktop (TCP-In)" or similar, specifically those allowing inbound connections on port 3389.
You should see a list of inbound rules, including any that explicitly allow RDP connections, along with their enabled status and the scope of allowed remote IP addresses.

steps.title: Harden Windows Firewall to Restrict RDP Access
steps.command_or_action: Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security
steps.explanation: Restricting RDP access to only trusted source IP addresses significantly reduces the attack surface, preventing unauthorized access attempts (T1110) and limiting potential lateral movement (T1563.002). This directly implements CIS 9.3.2 by ensuring inbound connections are blocked by default unless explicitly allowed from specific, authorized sources.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Inbound Rules
substeps: Open "Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security" by searching in the Start Menu or running `wf.msc`.
In the left pane, click on "Inbound Rules". Locate the "Remote Desktop (TCP-In)" rule (or the relevant RDP rule identified in Step 2).

Right-click the rule and select "Properties". Go to the "Scope" tab. Under "Remote IP address", select "These IP addresses" and add the specific trusted IP addresses (e.g., 10.128.1.176 if it's a legitimate source, or other management IPs) that are allowed to connect via RDP. Remove any "Any IP address" entries.
Click "Apply" and then "OK" to save the changes. You should see the "Remote IP address" scope for the RDP rule updated to only include the specified trusted IP addresses.

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Local Group Policy Editor
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (peer-to-peer) prevents the system from using broadcast queries for name resolution, which can reduce network noise and potential information leakage. This contributes to a more secure network environment by reducing the attack surface and aligns with control 18.3.4 (L1) for Windows Server.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Local Computer Policy > Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP
substeps: Open the "Local Group Policy Editor" by typing `gpedit.msc` in the Run dialog (Win+R) or searching in the Start Menu.
In the left pane, navigate to "Computer Configuration" > "Administrative Templates" > "Network" > "NetBIOS over TCP/IP".
Double-click the policy setting "NetBT NodeType configuration".

Select "Enabled" and then choose "P-node (recommended)" from the "Options" dropdown menu. Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "State" column for "NetBT NodeType configuration" change to "Enabled" and the setting description confirm "P-node".

steps.title: Disable LocalSystem NULL Session Fallback
steps.command_or_action: Local Group Policy Editor
steps.explanation: Disabling the "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback" policy prevents services running as LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session when attempting to access network resources. This reduces the risk of unauthorized access to network resources via NULL sessions, enhancing overall network security and directly addressing control 2.3.11.2 for Windows Server.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Local Computer Policy > Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open the "Local Group Policy Editor" by typing `gpedit.msc` in the Run dialog (Win+R) or searching in the Start Menu.
In the left pane, navigate to "Computer Configuration" > "Windows Settings" > "Security Settings" > "Local Policies" > "Security Options".
Double-click the policy setting "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback".
Select "Disabled". Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "Security Setting" column for "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback" change to "Disabled".

steps.title: Implement Account Lockout Policy for RDP Brute Force Protection
steps.command_or_action: Local Group Policy Editor
steps.explanation: Configuring an account lockout policy helps prevent brute-force attacks (T1110) against RDP by temporarily locking out user accounts after a specified number of failed login attempts. This is a critical component of system monitoring and protection as per NIST-SI-04, making it harder for attackers to guess credentials and achieve lateral movement (T1563.002).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Local Computer Policy > Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Account Policies > Account Lockout Policy
substeps: Open the "Local Group Policy Editor" by typing `gpedit.msc` in the Run dialog (Win+R) or searching in the Start Menu.
In the left pane, navigate to "Computer Configuration" > "Windows Settings" > "Security Settings" > "Account Policies" > "Account Lockout Policy".
Double-click "Account lockout threshold" and set it to a reasonable number (e.g., 5 invalid logon attempts) before an account is locked out.

Double-click "Account lockout duration" and set it (e.g., 30 minutes). Then, double-click "Reset account lockout counter after" and set it to the same duration (e.g., 30 minutes). Click "Apply" and "OK" for each. You should see the "Security Setting" column for these policies updated with the configured values.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the Windows Server (192.168.220.132) and any relevant Windows 11 client machines.
Understanding of legitimate RDP usage patterns within the environment.
remediation.verification_procedure: 1. Attempt to RDP from an unauthorized IP address to 192.168.220.132. The connection should be blocked by the firewall. 2. Attempt to RDP from an authorized IP address to 192.168.220.132. The connection should succeed. 3. From an authorized IP, intentionally fail RDP login attempts multiple times (e.g., 5 times) to verify the account lockout policy is active. The account should temporarily lock out. 4. Review Event Viewer logs (Event ID 4625 for failed logons) for failed RDP login attempts to confirm firewall blocks and account lockouts are being logged.
