remediation.executive_summary: A suspicious RDP connection was observed from 10.128.1.164 to 10.100.105.132 on port 3389. This activity could indicate an unauthorized access attempt or a brute-force attack (T1110). This remediation plan outlines steps to investigate the connection, harden the target system against RDP-based attacks, and enhance monitoring to detect similar future incidents.

steps.title: Investigate the suspicious RDP connection on the target server
steps.command_or_action: Review Windows Event Logs on 10.100.105.132 for RDP connection attempts and logon activity.
steps.explanation: This step aims to determine the nature of the suspicious RDP connection, identify if it was successful, and if any user accounts were compromised. This directly addresses the T1110 (Brute Force) MITRE ATT&CK ID by looking for signs of repeated failed login attempts or successful unauthorized access.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security
substeps: Open 'Event Viewer' on the target server (10.100.105.132).
Navigate to 'Windows Logs' and then select 'Security'.

Filter the logs for Event IDs related to RDP connections and logon attempts (e.g., 4624 for successful logon, 4625 for failed logon, 4776 for credential validation, 4648 for a logon attempt using explicit credentials). Look for entries originating from 10.128.1.164.
You should see a list of security events, allowing you to identify successful or failed RDP login attempts from the suspicious IP address.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to block inbound RDP connections on the target server
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy or local Windows Firewall to block inbound connections for the Domain Profile.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound RDP connections by default significantly reduces the attack surface, especially against brute-force attacks (T1110) targeting port 3389. This aligns with control 9.1.2 from the source controls.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall Properties > Domain Profile
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management Editor' (if managing via GPO) or 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' (if managing locally) on the target server.
Navigate to 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' > 'Windows Firewall Properties'.
Select the 'Domain Profile' tab.
Under 'State', locate 'Inbound connections' and set it to 'Block (default)'.
Click 'Apply' and 'OK'. You should see the 'Inbound connections' setting for the Domain Profile now explicitly showing 'Block (default)'.

steps.title: Ensure NetBT NodeType configuration is set to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy or local registry setting for NetBT NodeType.
steps.explanation: Setting the NetBT NodeType to P-node (peer-to-peer) prevents the system from using broadcast for name resolution, reducing exposure to NetBIOS-based enumeration and potential attacks. This aligns with control 18.3.4 (L1) from the source controls.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management Editor' (if managing via GPO) or 'Local Group Policy Editor' (if managing locally).
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Administrative Templates' > 'Network' > 'NetBIOS over TCP/IP'.
Double-click on the policy 'NetBT NodeType configuration'.
Set the policy to 'Enabled' and select 'P-node (recommended)' from the 'Options' dropdown.
Click 'Apply' and 'OK'. You should see the 'NetBT NodeType configuration' policy now listed as 'Enabled' with 'P-node' as the configured type.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy or local security policy to disable LocalSystem NULL session fallback.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents services running as LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session when attempting to access network resources. This hardens the system against potential information disclosure or unauthorized access via NULL sessions, aligning with control 2.3.11.2 from the source controls.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open 'Group Policy Management Editor' (if managing via GPO) or 'Local Security Policy' (if managing locally).
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Policies' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Security Options'.
Locate and double-click the policy 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'.
Set the policy to 'Disabled'.
Click 'Apply' and 'OK'. You should see the 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' policy now showing 'Disabled' in the policy list.

steps.title: Implement enhanced system monitoring for unauthorized connections
steps.command_or_action: Configure Cisco Meraki MX 250 firewall rules and alerts for RDP traffic.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring helps detect and respond to unauthorized RDP connection attempts, including brute-force attacks (T1110), in real-time. This aligns with NIST-SI-04 by monitoring for unauthorized network connections.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Meraki dashboard > Security & SD-WAN > Firewall > Layer 3 firewall rules
substeps: Log in to the Cisco Meraki dashboard.

Navigate to 'Security & SD-WAN' > 'Firewall' > 'Layer 3 firewall rules'. Review existing firewall rules to ensure that only authorized IPs or networks can initiate RDP (port 3389) connections to internal servers. If unauthorized RDP is allowed, create a new rule to block it.
Navigate to 'Network-wide' > 'Monitor' > 'Event log' and filter for 'Firewall' events related to RDP (port 3389) to identify blocked or allowed connections.

Navigate to 'Network-wide' > 'Alerts' and configure an alert for 'Firewall event log' entries that match RDP traffic (port 3389) from unexpected sources or to sensitive internal IPs. You should receive a confirmation that the alert rule has been saved and is active.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server (10.100.105.132).
Administrative access to Group Policy Management (if managing via GPO).
Administrative access to the Cisco Meraki dashboard.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the remediation steps, verify that RDP connections from unauthorized sources are blocked and that monitoring alerts are configured to detect future suspicious activity. Attempt an RDP connection from a known unauthorized IP to confirm it is blocked by the firewall.

remediation.executive_summary: An RDP connection from an unknown source IP was observed, indicating potential unauthorized access or a service requiring enhanced monitoring. This remediation plan focuses on investigating the specific RDP event, hardening the Windows Server's RDP and network security configurations, and implementing robust monitoring to detect and prevent future suspicious RDP activities. These steps align with NIST guidelines for system monitoring and specific Windows security baselines.

steps.title: Investigate the Observed RDP Connection
steps.command_or_action: Review Windows Event Logs on the destination server (192.168.220.132) for RDP connection events.
steps.explanation: This step is critical to understand the nature of the observed RDP connection. By reviewing logs, you can determine if the connection was legitimate, identify the user account involved, and check for any associated suspicious activities like failed login attempts, which aligns with NIST-SI-04's requirement to detect attacks and unauthorized connections.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Windows Logs > Security
substeps: On the destination server (192.168.220.132), open 'Event Viewer' by searching for it in the Start menu.
Navigate to 'Windows Logs' and then select 'Security'.
Filter the logs for Event ID 4624 (successful logon) and 4625 (failed logon) during the time the RDP connection from 10.128.1.164 was observed.
Examine the 'Source Network Address' field within these events to identify connections originating from 10.128.1.164.
Note the 'Account Name' for successful logons and the 'Failure Reason' for failed logons.
You should see detailed entries for RDP connection attempts, including source IP, user account, and success/failure status.

steps.title: Ensure RDP Connection Logging is Enabled
steps.command_or_action: Configure Group Policy to enable auditing for RDP connections.
steps.explanation: Enabling comprehensive auditing for RDP connections ensures that all attempts, successful or failed, are logged. This is a fundamental aspect of system monitoring as per NIST-SI-04, providing essential data for future investigations and anomaly detection.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Local Group Policy Editor > Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > Audit Policies > Logon/Logoff
substeps: Open 'Local Group Policy Editor' by typing 'gpedit.msc' in the Run dialog (Win+R).
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Advanced Audit Policy Configuration' > 'Audit Policies' > 'Logon/Logoff'.
Double-click on 'Audit Logon' and 'Audit Logoff' policies. Check 'Configure the following audit events' and ensure 'Success' and 'Failure' checkboxes are marked.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Audit Policy'. Ensure 'Audit logon events' is set to 'Success and Failure'.
Apply the changes and run 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated Command Prompt to ensure the new policy settings are applied immediately.
You should see 'Success and Failure' selected for 'Audit Logon' and 'Audit Logoff' policies, indicating that RDP connection events will be logged.

steps.title: Harden Windows Firewall for RDP Inbound Connections
steps.command_or_action: Configure Windows Defender Firewall to block inbound connections by default and create specific rules for authorized RDP access.
steps.explanation: Blocking inbound connections by default significantly reduces the attack surface, preventing unauthorized network connections as required by NIST-SI-04 and directly implementing controls 9.1.2 and 9.3.2. Specific rules should only allow RDP from trusted source IPs.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security > Inbound Rules
substeps: Open 'Windows Defender Firewall with Advanced Security' by searching for it in the Start menu.
In the left pane, click on 'Windows Defender Firewall Properties'.
For each profile (Domain, Private, Public), ensure 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block (default)'. Click 'OK'.
In the left pane, click 'Inbound Rules'. Locate the existing 'Remote Desktop (TCP-In)' rules.

Right-click on the 'Remote Desktop (TCP-In)' rule(s) that allow RDP and select 'Properties'. Go to the 'Scope' tab and under 'Remote IP address', select 'These IP addresses' and add only the specific, authorized source IP addresses (e.g., 10.128.1.164 if it's legitimate) that should be allowed to connect via RDP. Remove any 'Any IP address' entries.
You should see the firewall profiles set to 'Block (default)' for inbound connections and the RDP inbound rules explicitly listing only authorized source IP addresses.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Set the Group Policy 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled'.
steps.explanation: Disabling NULL session fallback prevents anonymous access to system resources, which can be exploited by attackers to enumerate user accounts or share information. This is a direct hardening measure to reduce the attack surface and prevent unauthorized use, aligning with control 2.3.11.2.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Local Group Policy Editor > Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open 'Local Group Policy Editor' by typing 'gpedit.msc' in the Run dialog (Win+R).
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Windows Settings' > 'Security Settings' > 'Local Policies' > 'Security Options'.
Locate and double-click the policy 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'.
Select the 'Disabled' option.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. Run 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated Command Prompt.
You should see the 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' policy status as 'Disabled'.

steps.title: Configure 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'P-node'
steps.command_or_action: Set the Group Policy 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)'.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBT NodeType to P-node (Point-to-Point) ensures that NetBIOS name resolution relies primarily on a NetBIOS Name Server (WINS) and does not broadcast name queries, reducing the risk of NetBIOS-related attacks and information leakage on the network. This aligns with control 18.3.4.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Local Group Policy Editor > Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP
substeps: Open 'Local Group Policy Editor' by typing 'gpedit.msc' in the Run dialog (Win+R).
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration' > 'Administrative Templates' > 'Network' > 'NetBIOS over TCP/IP'.
Locate and double-click the policy 'NetBT NodeType configuration'.
Select 'Enabled' and then choose 'P-node (recommended)' from the 'Options:' dropdown menu.
Click 'Apply' and then 'OK'. Run 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated Command Prompt.
You should see the 'NetBT NodeType configuration' policy status as 'Enabled' with 'P-node (recommended)' selected.

steps.title: Implement Ongoing RDP Monitoring and Alerting
steps.command_or_action: Create custom Event Viewer views and consider integrating with a centralized logging solution for RDP activity.
steps.explanation: Proactive monitoring and alerting for RDP connections are essential for detecting suspicious activity in real-time, as emphasized by NIST-SI-04. This allows for rapid response to potential brute-force attacks (T1110) or RDP hijacking attempts (T1563.002).
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Event Viewer > Custom Views
substeps: Open 'Event Viewer' and navigate to 'Custom Views'. Right-click and select 'Create Custom View...'.
In the 'Create Custom View' dialog, select 'By log' and choose 'Security'. In the 'Event IDs' field, enter '4624, 4625, 4634, 4647, 4776, 4820, 5140, 5142' (common RDP and logon/logoff events).

Optionally, filter by 'Source' (e.g., 'Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing') and 'Keywords' (e.g., 'Audit Success', 'Audit Failure'). Give the custom view a descriptive name like 'RDP Activity Monitoring'.

Regularly review this custom view for unusual patterns, such as multiple failed login attempts from a single source IP, successful logins from unexpected locations, or RDP sessions outside of normal business hours.
If a centralized logging solution (e.g., SIEM) is available in the environment, configure Windows Event Forwarding to send these RDP-related security events to it for automated alerting.
You should see a new custom view in Event Viewer populated with relevant RDP and logon/logoff events, providing a focused dashboard for monitoring.

prerequisites: Administrative access to the target Windows Server (192.168.220.132).
Understanding of network topology and legitimate RDP source IP addresses.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying the remediation steps, attempt an RDP connection from an unauthorized IP address and verify that it is blocked by the firewall. Attempt a legitimate RDP connection and verify that successful logon events (Event ID 4624) are logged in the Event Viewer's Security log and appear in the custom RDP monitoring view. Confirm that the specified Group Policy settings are applied by re-checking them in 'gpedit.msc' or by running 'gpresult /h C:\gpresult.html' and reviewing the generated report.

This remediation plan outlines steps to investigate the specific RDP activity, harden Windows Firewall configurations to restrict RDP access, and apply additional security configurations to Windows Servers to reduce the attack surface. These measures will help prevent future unauthorized RDP connections and improve overall system security.

steps.title: Investigate Observed RDP Connection
steps.command_or_action: Review security logs and network flow data for RDP connections.
steps.explanation: This step aims to determine the nature and origin of the suspicious RDP connection, identify if it was legitimate or malicious, and assess the extent of any potential compromise. This directly addresses the T1110 finding by looking for evidence of brute force or successful RDP access.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Microsoft 365 Defender > Incidents & alerts > Alerts
substeps: Navigate to the Microsoft 365 Defender portal and select "Incidents & alerts" then "Alerts" from the left-hand navigation pane.
Filter alerts for RDP-related activities or search for connections involving the source IP '10.128.1.176' and destination IP '10.100.104.132' on port '3389'.

Examine the details of the observed connection, including user accounts involved, connection duration, and any associated authentication failures, to determine if it was legitimate or indicative of a brute-force attempt.
You should see detailed log entries or alerts related to the RDP connection, indicating success or failure, and user context.

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections for Domain Profile
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Windows Firewall: Domain: Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)' via Group Policy.
steps.explanation: This hardening step prevents unauthorized inbound connections, including RDP, to systems joined to the domain, significantly reducing the attack surface for brute-force attacks (T1110) and other network-based exploits.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall Properties > Domain Profile
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management Editor" on a domain controller or a management workstation with RSAT installed.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' in the console tree.
Right-click "Windows Firewall with Advanced Security" in the left pane and select "Properties".
Go to the "Domain Profile" tab. Under "State", ensure "Inbound connections" is set to "Block (default)".
Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "Inbound connections" setting for the Domain Profile now explicitly showing "Block (default)".

steps.title: Configure Windows Firewall to Block Inbound Connections for Public Profile
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Windows Firewall: Public: Inbound connections' to 'Block (default)' via Group Policy.
steps.explanation: This step extends the firewall hardening to systems operating on public networks, ensuring that even if a system is not on the domain, it is protected from unauthorized inbound connections, including RDP.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall Properties > Public Profile
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management Editor" on a domain controller or a management workstation with RSAT installed.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' in the console tree.
Right-click "Windows Firewall with Advanced Security" in the left pane and select "Properties".
Go to the "Public Profile" tab. Under "State", ensure "Inbound connections" is set to "Block (default)".
Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "Inbound connections" setting for the Public Profile now explicitly showing "Block (default)".

steps.title: Configure NetBT NodeType to P-node
steps.command_or_action: Set 'NetBT NodeType configuration' to 'Enabled: P-node (recommended)' via Group Policy.
steps.explanation: Configuring NetBIOS over TCP/IP (NetBT) to P-node (peer-to-peer) prevents systems from relying on broadcast for name resolution, reducing the risk of NetBIOS-related attacks like spoofing or information disclosure. This is a general hardening measure for Windows Server.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management Editor" on a domain controller or a management workstation with RSAT installed.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Network > NetBIOS over TCP/IP' in the console tree.
Locate and double-click the policy setting "NetBT NodeType configuration" in the right pane.
Select "Enabled" and then choose "P-node (recommended)" from the "Node Type" dropdown menu.
Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "NetBT NodeType configuration" policy now showing "Enabled" with "P-node (recommended)" as its setting.

steps.title: Disable 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback'
steps.command_or_action: Set 'Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback' to 'Disabled' via Group Policy.
steps.explanation: Disabling this setting prevents services running as LocalSystem from falling back to a NULL session when attempting to access network resources. This reduces the risk of unauthorized access or information disclosure through unauthenticated NULL sessions.
steps.ui_breadcrumb: Group Policy Management Editor > Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options
substeps: Open "Group Policy Management Editor" on a domain controller or a management workstation with RSAT installed.
Navigate to 'Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > Security Options' in the console tree.
Locate and double-click the policy setting "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback" in the right pane.
Select "Disabled".
Click "Apply" and "OK". You should see the "Network security: Allow LocalSystem NULL session fallback" policy now showing "Disabled".

prerequisites: Administrative access to a Domain Controller or a workstation with Group Policy Management Editor and RSAT installed.
Access to Microsoft 365 Defender portal or Azure Monitor for log investigation.
Network connectivity to the affected Windows Servers.
remediation.verification_procedure: After applying Group Policies, force a Group Policy update on target servers using 'gpupdate /force' in an elevated command prompt. Verify the firewall settings by checking the 'Windows Firewall with Advanced Security' console on a target server and confirming 'Inbound connections' is set to 'Block' for both Domain and Public profiles. Verify the NetBT NodeType by checking the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NetBT\Parameters and looking for NodeType (should be 0x2 for P-node). Verify the NULL session fallback setting by checking the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa and looking for AllowLsaNullSessionFallback (should be 0). Monitor network logs for any further suspicious RDP connections from the identified source or to the identified destination.

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

These rules are effective for identifying the use of RDP in the environment and can be added to your IDS/IPS or log files. The first rule is for a connection to the RDP port (3389) and the second rule is for a connection to a legitimate RDP session.

Rule 1: 101281.001 – RDP on non-standard port 10128.1.0: The threat actor is attempting to connect to a non-standard port. This rule will alert on any connection to 1012

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Boundary Protection

Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system; Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; and Connect to external networks or systems only through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Managed interfaces include gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis, virtualization systems, or encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture. Subnetworks that are physically or logically separated from internal networks are referred to as demilitarized zones or DMZs. Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational systems includes restricting external web traffic to designated web servers within managed interfaces, prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses, and prohibiting internal traffic that appears to be spoofing external addresses. [SP 800-189](#f5edfe51-d1f2-422e-9b27-5d0e90b49c72) provides additional information on source address validation techniques to prevent ingress and egress of traffic with spoofed addresses. Commercial telecommunications services are provided by network components and consolidated management systems shared by customers. These services may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions. Boundary protection may be implemented as a common control for all or part of an organizational network such that the boundary to be protected is greater than a system-specific boundary (i.e., an authorization boundary). communications at external managed interfaces to the system are monitored; communications at external managed interfaces to the system are controlled; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are monitored; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are controlled; subnetworks for publicly accessible system components are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; external networks or systems are only connected to through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Least Functionality

Configure the system to provide only [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Prohibit or restrict the use of the following functions, ports, protocols, software, and/or services: [ASSIGNMENT]. Systems provide a wide variety of functions and services. Some of the functions and services routinely provided by default may not be necessary to support essential organizational missions, functions, or operations. Additionally, it is sometimes convenient to provide multiple services from a single system component, but doing so increases risk over limiting the services provided by that single component. Where feasible, organizations limit component functionality to a single function per component. Organizations consider removing unused or unnecessary software and disabling unused or unnecessary physical and logical ports and protocols to prevent unauthorized connection of components, transfer of information, and tunneling. Organizations employ network scanning tools, intrusion detection and prevention systems, and end-point protection technologies, such as firewalls and host-based intrusion detection systems, to identify and prevent the use of prohibited functions, protocols, ports, and services. best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System and information integrity policy system and information integrity procedures procedures addressing system monitoring tools and techniques continuous monitoring strategy facility diagram/layout system design documentation system monitoring tools and techniques documentation locations within the system where monitoring devices are deployed system configuration settings and associated documentation system security plan other relevant documents or records System/network administrators organizational personnel with information security responsibilities organizational personnel installing, configuring, and/or maintaining the system organizational personnel responsible for monitoring the system Organizational processes for system monitoring mechanisms supporting and/or implementing system monitoring capabilities

findings.description: Connection from 10.128.1.176 to 192.168.220.132 on port 3389 (RDP). This port is commonly associated with attacks or services that should be monitored.

metadata.technical_summary: A connection from 10.128.1.176 to 192.168.220.132 on port 3389 (RDP) was observed, indicating potential suspicious RDP service access or attack activity.

mitre_mapping.raw_model_output:
These rules are effective for identifying the use of RDP in the environment and can be added to your IDS/IPS or log files. The first rule is for a connection to the RDP port (3389) and the second rule is for a connection to a legitimate RDP session.

### Rules:
Rule 1: 101281.001 – RDP on non-standard port 10128.1.0: The threat actor is attempting to connect to a non-standard port. This rule will alert on any connection to 1012

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Boundary Protection

Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system; Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; and Connect to external networks or systems only through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Managed interfaces include gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis, virtualization systems, or encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture. Subnetworks that are physically or logically separated from internal networks are referred to as demilitarized zones or DMZs. Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational systems includes restricting external web traffic to designated web servers within managed interfaces, prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses, and prohibiting internal traffic that appears to be spoofing external addresses. [SP 800-189](#f5edfe51-d1f2-422e-9b27-5d0e90b49c72) provides additional information on source address validation techniques to prevent ingress and egress of traffic with spoofed addresses. Commercial telecommunications services are provided by network components and consolidated management systems shared by customers. These services may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions. Boundary protection may be implemented as a common control for all or part of an organizational network such that the boundary to be protected is greater than a system-specific boundary (i.e., an authorization boundary). communications at external managed interfaces to the system are monitored; communications at external managed interfaces to the system are controlled; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are monitored; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are controlled; subnetworks for publicly accessible system components are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; external networks or systems are only connected to through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Least Functionality

Configure the system to provide only [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Prohibit or restrict the use of the following functions, ports, protocols, software, and/or services: [ASSIGNMENT]. Systems provide a wide variety of functions and services. Some of the functions and services routinely provided by default may not be necessary to support essential organizational missions, functions, or operations. Additionally, it is sometimes convenient to provide multiple services from a single system component, but doing so increases risk over limiting the services provided by that single component. Where feasible, organizations limit component functionality to a single function per component. Organizations consider removing unused or unnecessary software and disabling unused or unnecessary physical and logical ports and protocols to prevent unauthorized connection of components, transfer of information, and tunneling. Organizations employ network scanning tools, intrusion detection and prevention systems, and end-point protection technologies, such as firewalls and host-based intrusion detection systems, to identify and prevent the use of prohibited functions, protocols, ports, and services. Least functionality can also be achieved as part of the fundamental design and development of the system (see [SA-8](#sa-8), [SC-2](#sc-2) , and [SC-3](#sc-3)). the system is configured to provide only [ASSIGNMENT]; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted; the use of [ASSIGNMENT] is prohibited or restricted. best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). mitre_mapping.raw_model_output:
These are valid. The first has a very high degree of similarity and the second has a moderate degree of similarity.

### Categories:
- IT Security
- NetworkSecurity
- SystemCredentialsOperating
- SystemProcessSystem
- SystemRemoteServices

### Mitre-Org:
TA500

### Details:
The first MITRE ATT&CK technique, T1110, is a command and control (C2) node, while the second, T1563.002, is a service or tool. TA

System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). These rules are effective for identifying the use of RDP in the environment and can be added to your IDS/IPS or log files. The first rule is for a connection to the RDP port (3389) and the second rule is for a connection to a legitimate RDP session.

### Rules:
Rule 1: 101281.001 – RDP on non-standard port 10128.1.0: The threat actor is attempting to connect to a non-standard port. This rule will alert on any connection to 1012

best_control.remediation_steps:
System Monitoring

Monitor the system to detect: Attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with the following monitoring objectives: [ASSIGNMENT] ; and Unauthorized local, network, and remote connections; Identify unauthorized use of the system through the following techniques and methods: [ASSIGNMENT]; Invoke internal monitoring capabilities or deploy monitoring devices: Strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; and At ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; Analyze detected events and anomalies; Adjust the level of system monitoring activity when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; Obtain legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities; and Provide [ASSIGNMENT] to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. System monitoring includes external and internal monitoring. External monitoring includes the observation of events occurring at external interfaces to the system. Internal monitoring includes the observation of events occurring within the system. Organizations monitor systems by observing audit activities in real time or by observing other system aspects such as access patterns, characteristics of access, and other actions. The monitoring objectives guide and inform the determination of the events. System monitoring capabilities are achieved through a variety of tools and techniques, including intrusion detection and prevention systems, malicious code protection software, scanning tools, audit record monitoring software, and network monitoring software. Depending on the security architecture, the distribution and configuration of monitoring devices may impact throughput at key internal and external boundaries as well as at other locations across a network due to the introduction of network throughput latency. If throughput management is needed, such devices are strategically located and deployed as part of an established organization-wide security architecture. Strategic locations for monitoring devices include selected perimeter locations and near key servers and server farms that support critical applications. Monitoring devices are typically employed at the managed interfaces associated with controls [SC-7](#sc-7) and [AC-17](#ac-17) . The information collected is a function of the organizational monitoring objectives and the capability of systems to support such objectives. Specific types of transactions of interest include Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) traffic that bypasses HTTP proxies. System monitoring is an integral part of organizational continuous monitoring and incident response programs, and output from system monitoring serves as input to those programs. System monitoring requirements, including the need for specific types of system monitoring, may be referenced in other controls (e.g., [AC-2g](#ac-2_smt.g), [AC-2(7)](#ac-2.7), [AC-2(12)(a)](#ac-2.12_smt.a), [AC-17(1)](#ac-17.1), [AU-13](#au-13), [AU-13(1)](#au-13.1), [AU-13(2)](#au-13.2), [CM-3f](#cm-3_smt.f), [CM-6d](#cm-6_smt.d), [MA-3a](#ma-3_smt.a), [MA-4a](#ma-4_smt.a), [SC-5(3)(b)](#sc-5.3_smt.b), [SC-7a](#sc-7_smt.a), [SC-7(24)(b)](#sc-7.24_smt.b), [SC-18b](#sc-18_smt.b), [SC-43b](#sc-43_smt.b) ). Adjustments to levels of system monitoring are based on law enforcement information, intelligence information, or other sources of information. The legality of system monitoring activities is based on applicable laws, executive orders, directives, regulations, policies, standards, and guidelines. the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks in accordance with [ASSIGNMENT]; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized local connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized network connections; the system is monitored to detect unauthorized remote connections; unauthorized use of the system is identified through [ASSIGNMENT]; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed strategically within the system to collect organization-determined essential information; internal monitoring capabilities are invoked or monitoring devices are deployed at ad hoc locations within the system to track specific types of transactions of interest to the organization; detected events are analyzed; detected anomalies are analyzed; the level of system monitoring activity is adjusted when there is a change in risk to organizational operations and assets, individuals, other organizations, or the Nation; a legal opinion regarding system monitoring activities is obtained; [ASSIGNMENT] is provided to [ASSIGNMENT] [ASSIGNMENT]. Boundary Protection

Monitor and control communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal managed interfaces within the system; Implement subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; and Connect to external networks or systems only through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. Managed interfaces include gateways, routers, firewalls, guards, network-based malicious code analysis, virtualization systems, or encrypted tunnels implemented within a security architecture. Subnetworks that are physically or logically separated from internal networks are referred to as demilitarized zones or DMZs. Restricting or prohibiting interfaces within organizational systems includes restricting external web traffic to designated web servers within managed interfaces, prohibiting external traffic that appears to be spoofing internal addresses, and prohibiting internal traffic that appears to be spoofing external addresses. [SP 800-189](#f5edfe51-d1f2-422e-9b27-5d0e90b49c72) provides additional information on source address validation techniques to prevent ingress and egress of traffic with spoofed addresses. Commercial telecommunications services are provided by network components and consolidated management systems shared by customers. These services may also include third party-provided access lines and other service elements. Such services may represent sources of increased risk despite contract security provisions. Boundary protection may be implemented as a common control for all or part of an organizational network such that the boundary to be protected is greater than a system-specific boundary (i.e., an authorization boundary). communications at external managed interfaces to the system are monitored; communications at external managed interfaces to the system are controlled; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are monitored; communications at key internal managed interfaces within the system are controlled; subnetworks for publicly accessible system components are [ASSIGNMENT] separated from internal organizational networks; external networks or systems are only connected to through managed interfaces consisting of boundary protection devices arranged in accordance with an organizational security and privacy architecture. control.control_id: 2.3.10.4 (L1) Ensure 'Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users' is set to 'Disabled' (Automated)
control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:EveryoneIncludesAnonymous
control.remediation_steps: To establish the recommended configuration via GP, set the following UI path to Disabled: Computer Configuration\Policies\Windows Settings\Security Settings\Local Policies\Security Options\Network access: Let Everyone permissions apply to anonymous users

control.control_id: 2.3.10.3 (L1) Ensure 'Network access: Do not allow anonymous enumeration of SAM accounts and shares' is set to 'Enabled' (MS only) (Automated)
control.audit_procedure: Navigate to the UI Path articulated in the Remediation section and confirm it is set as prescribed. This group policy setting is backed by the following registry location: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa:RestrictAnonymous
